metadata.summary: A package for rapidly developing "nice" Python bindings to C libraries, using `cffi`

  logo: |-

> ```
> alt
>
> Travis CI
> ```

# {{ logo }} NiceLib

NiceLib is a package for rapidly developing "nice" Python bindings to C libraries, using `cffi`.

NiceLib accomplishes this in two main ways: first, it converts header files (macros and all) into a
format usable by `cffi` (i.e. it preprocesses them); second, it provides an API for quickly and
cleanly defining pythonic mid-level interfaces that wrap low-level libraries.

For further information, documentation, examples, and more, see our page on
[ReadTheDocs](http://nicelib.readthedocs.org/).

For contributing, reporting issues, and providing feedback, see our
[GitHub page](https://github.com/mabuchilab/NiceLib).

## Installing

NiceLib is available on PyPI:

```
$ pip install nicelib
```

If you would like to use the development version, download and extract a zip of the source from our
[GitHub page](https://github.com/mabuchilab/NiceLib) or clone it using git. Now install:

```
$ cd /path/to/NiceLib
$ pip install .
```

modules.name: nicelib.build
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright 2016-2018 Nate Bogdanowicz

functions.name: nicelib.build.build_lib
documentation:
Build a low-level Python wrapper of a C lib

# Parameters

header_info

: Path to header file. If None, uses only the header source given by `preamble`.

  Paths can use `os.environ`, as described below. Info is provided in the form of a dict
  which must contain a 'header' key whose value is either a str containing a single header
  name, or a tuple of such strings.

  There are also some other optional entries:

  The `'path'` value must be a str or tuple of strs that are directories which will be
  searched for the given header(s). Any headers that are specified with a leading slash are
  considered absolute and are not affected by this path.

  The `'predef'` value is a str which is the name or path of a header file which will be
  used to populate the predefined preprocessor macros that are ordinarily provided by the
  compiler on a per-system basis. If provided, this overrides the default header that NiceLib
  uses for your system.

lib_name

: Name of compiled library file, e.g. `'mylib.dll'`

module_name

: Name of module to create. Must be in the format `'_*lib'`, e.g. `'_mylib'`

filedir

: Path indicating the directory where the generated module will be saved. If `filedir`
  points to an existing file, that file's directory is used. Usually you would pass the
  `__file__` attribute from your build module.

ignored_headers

: Names of headers to ignore; `#include`s containing these will be skipped.

ignore_system_headers

: If True, skip inclusion of headers specified with angle brackets, e.g.
  `#include <stdarg.h>` Header files specified with double quotes are processed as ususal.
  Default is False.

preamble

: C source to insert before the headers specified by `header_info`. Useful for including
  typedefs that would otherwise be hard to reach due to an end user missing headers.

token_hooks

: Token hook functions. See `process_headers()` for more info.

ast_hooks

: AST hook functions. See `process_headers()` for more info.

hook_groups

: Hook groups. See `process_headers()` for more info.

debug_file

: File to write a partially-processed header to just before it is parsed by `pycparser`.
  Useful for debugging the preprocessor when `pycparser`'s parser chokes on its output.

logbuf

: IO buffer to write() common log output to. By default this output will logged using the
  `logging` stdlib module, at the `info` log level. You can use `sys.stdout` to perform
  ordinary printing.

load_dump_file

: Save the list of tokens resulting from preprocessing to 'token_dump.pkl'. See save_dump_file
  for more info.

save_dump_file

: Ignore `header_paths` and load the already-preprocessed tokens from 'token_dump.pkl'. This
  can significantly speed up your turnaround time when debugging really large header sets
  when writing and debugging hooks.

pack: int

: Forwared to `FFI.cdef`. Controls the packing of structs if necessary. This has to be done
  manually since CFFI ignores `#pragma pack` and gcc directives. See the CFFI documentation
  for more information.

override: bool

: Forwarded to `FFI.cdef`. If True, allows repeated declarations; the final declaration will
  override any others. Otherwise, repeated declarations are treated as an error.

# Notes

`header_info` and `lib_name` can each be a dict that maps from a platform to the
corresponding path or name, allowing cross-platform support. The keys are matched against
`sys.platform` and can use globbing, i.e. `'linux*'` will match anything starting with
`'linux'`.

The path or paths provided by `header_info` may use items in `os.environ`. For example,
`'{PROGRAMFILES}\\header.h'` will be formatted with `os.environ['PROGRAMFILES']`.

modules.name: nicelib
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright 2016-2018 Nate Bogdanowicz

functions.name: nicelib.load_lib
documentation:
Load a low-level lib module, building it first if required.

If `name` is `'foo'`, tries to import a module named `_foolib`. If the module can't be located,
`load_lib` tries to build it.

# Parameters

name

: The name of the library. A name of `'foo'` would try to import the module `_foolib`.

pkg

: The package within which to look for the module. If None, looks for a top-level module.

dir

: A directory within which to search for the module, before checking the system path. Useful
  when working with local modules not within a package.

builder

: The name of the module whose `build()` function is used to generate `_foolib.py` By
  default, it is assumed to be `_build_foo` (where 'foo' is the value of `name`).

kwargs

: Keyword args to be passed to `build()`.

# Returns

lib : `LibInfo`

classes.name: nicelib.LibInfo
documentation: Opaque object containing library information

modules.name: nicelib.util
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright 2016-2018 Nate Bogdanowicz

functions.name: nicelib.util.to_tuple
documentation: Convert value to a tuple, or if it is a string or dict, wrap it in a tuple

functions.name: nicelib.util.suppress
documentation: Context manager to suppress specified exceptions
For Python 2 support

functions.name: nicelib.util.handle_header_path
documentation:
Find the paths to the specified headers and verify they exist

`path` may take a few forms. It may be just a string, in which case it specifies the absolute
path to a header file.

Otherwise, it must be in the form of a "platform" dict, which maps platform-specific strings
to "header" dicts. Each "header" dict must contain a 'header' entry, which is a string or tuple
of strings indicating header paths. Optionally you may include a 'path' entry, which specifies
a list of directories where the headers may be located.

functions.name: nicelib.util.find_header
documentation:
Resolve the path of a header_name

Supports inclusion of environment variables in header and dir names. If `header_name` is a
relative path (e.g. simply a filename), it is searched for relative to each of the paths in
`include_dirs`, otherwise `include_dirs` is ignored and the absolute path is used directly.
Raises an exception if the header cannot be found.

functions.name: nicelib.util.handle_lib_name
documentation:
Find the path to the library

`lib_name` can be specified directly as a string (or sequence of strings), or within a
"platform" dict. If multiple libs are specified, the first one found will be returned.

functions.name: nicelib.nicelib.sig_pattern
documentation:
Create a dict of Sigs by expanding a set of patterns.

Parameters
----------
sig_patterns : sequence of pairs (`pattern`, `sig`)
    Each `sig` is an ordinary sig, and `pattern` is a string pattern which will be completed
    with each of the names given, using `str.format()`
names : sequence of strings

functions.name: nicelib.nicelib.ret_return
documentation: Append the return value to the wrapped function's return values.

functions.name: nicelib.nicelib.ret_ignore
documentation: Ignore the return value.

functions.name: nicelib.nicelib._wrap_inarg
documentation:
Convert an input arg to the argtype required by the underlying C function

`argtype` is the ffi.CType to which `arg` should be converted.

Converts a string to `char *` or `char[]`
Converts a number to its corresponding CType
                  or a new pointer to the number
Converts a numpy array to the correct numeric pointer (only flat arrays for now)
Creates a pointer to arg if argtype is a typeof(arg) pointer
Otherwise, tries to cast arg to argtype via ffi.cast()

If `argtype` is not a CType, returns `arg` unmodified

methods.name: nicelib.nicelib.Sig.__init__
documentation:
Create a signature specification.

Parameters
----------
arg_strs : strings
    Strings defining the input-output signature of the underlying C function being wrapped.
    There is a one-to-one correspondence between arg strings and the C function's args.
flags
    Flags(settings) to be applied to this function.

methods.name: nicelib.nicelib.Sig.__call__
documentation: Signature decorator for hybrid functions

classes.name: nicelib.nicelib.RetHandler
documentation: Decorator class for creating return handlers

classes.name: nicelib.nicelib.NiceObject
documentation:
Base class for object-like mid-level library wrappers

# Attributes

\_prefix\_

: Prefix(es) to strip from the library "method" names. E.g. If the library has functions
  named like `SDK_Obj_Func()`, you can set `_prefix_` to `'SDK_Obj_'`, and access them
  as `Func()`. If more than one prefix is given, they are tried in order for each signature
  until the appropraite function is found.

\_ret\_

: `RetHandler` to handle the return values of each C function. By default, the return value
  will be appended to the end of the Python return values. The return handler function takes
  the C function's return value (often an error/success code) as its only argument. If the
  wrapper returns a non-None value, it will be appended to the wrapped function's return
  values.

  NiceLib defines the simple handlers `ret_return()` and `ret_ignore()` for convenience.

\_struct_maker\_

: Function that is called to create an FFI struct of the given type. Mainly useful for
  odd libraries that require you to always fill out some field of the struct, like its size
  in bytes

\_buflen\_

: The default length for buffers. This can be overridden on a per-argument basis in the
  argument's spec string, e.g `'len=64'` will make a 64-byte buffer.

\_use_numpy\_

: If true, convert output args marked as 'arr' to `numpy` arrays. Obviously requires
  `numpy` to be installed.

classes.name: nicelib.nicelib.NiceObjectDef
documentation: WARNING uses some stack frame hackery; should probably make use of this syntax optional

classes.name: nicelib.nicelib.NiceLib

class_variables.name: nicelib.nicelib.NiceLib._ffi
documentation: MUST be filled in by subclass

class_variables.name: nicelib.nicelib.NiceLib._ffilib
documentation: MUST be filled in by subclass

Base class for mid-level library wrappers

Provides a nice interface for quickly defining mid-level library wrappers. You define a
subclass for each specific library (.dll/.so file).

# Attributes

\_info\_

: A `LibInfo` object that contains access to the underlying library and macros. Required
  (unless you are using the old-style `_ffi_`, `_ffilib_`, and `_defs_` attributes)

\_ffi\_

: FFI instance variable. Required if not using `_info_`

\_ffilib\_

: FFI library opened with `dlopen()`. Required if not using `_info_`.

\_defs\_

: `dict` containing the Python-equivalent macros defined in the header file(s). Optional and
  only used if not using `_info_`.

\_prefix\_

: Prefix(es) to strip from the library function names. E.g. If the library has functions named
  like `SDK_Func()`, you can set `_prefix_` to `'SDK_'`, and access them as `Func()`.
  If more than one prefix is given, they are tried in order for each signature until the
  appropraite function is found.

\_ret\_

: `RetHandler` to handle the return values of each C function. By default, the return value
  will be appended to the end of the Python return values. The return handler function takes
  the C function's return value (often an error/success code) as its only argument. If the
  wrapper returns a non-None value, it will be appended to the wrapped function's return
  values.

  NiceLib defines the simple handlers `ret_return()` and `ret_ignore()` for convenience.

\_struct_maker\_

: Function that is called to create an FFI struct of the given type. Mainly useful for
  odd libraries that require you to always fill out some field of the struct, like its size
  in bytes

\_buflen\_

: The default length for buffers. This can be overridden on a per-argument basis in the
  argument's spec string, e.g `'len=64'` will make a 64-byte buffer.

\_use_numpy\_

: If true, convert output args marked as 'arr' to `numpy` arrays. Obviously requires
  `numpy` to be installed.

Copyright 2016-2019 Nate Bogdanowicz

documentation: Convert C expression source str to a Python source str

functions.name: nicelib.process.src_to_c_ast
documentation: Convert C expression source str to a c_ast expression node

functions.name: nicelib.process.to_py_ast
documentation: Convert a c_ast expression into a Python ast object

documentation: Convert a c_ast expression into a Python source code string list
TODO: Convert this to using a generator pattern like CGenerator?

functions.name: nicelib.process.process_headers
documentation:
Preprocess header(s) and split into a cleaned header and macros

# Parameters

header_paths

: Paths of the headers

ignored_headers

: Names of headers to ignore; `#include`s containing these will be skipped.

ignore_system_headers

: If True, skip inclusion of headers specified with angle brackets, e.g. `#include
  <stdarg.h>` Header files specified with double quotes are processed as ususal. Default is
  False.

debug_file

: File to write a partially-processed header to just before it is parsed by `pycparser`.
  Useful for debugging the preprocessor when `pycparser`'s parser chokes on its output.

preamble

: C source to insert before the headers specified by `header_paths`. Useful for including
  typedefs that would otherwise be hard to reach due to an end user missing headers.
  token_hooks : sequence of functions
  Hook functions to be run on the already preprocessed token stream. Each function should
  accept and return a sequence of `Token`s. These are applied after any builtin token hooks.

ast_hooks

: Hook functions to be run on chunks of the header's C AST. After preprocessing and running
  the token hooks, the tokens are grouped and joined to form a sequence of chunks called
  "external declarations" (declarations, typedefs, and function definitions). Each chunk is
  parsed by `pycparser`, then passed through the list of AST hook functions to transform it.
  These are applied after any builtin AST hooks.

  AST hook functions take the parsed `FileAST` and the persistent `CParser` instance as
  arguments, and return a transformed `FileAST`. It is useful to have a reference to the
  parser to add phony typedefs if necessary.

hook_groups

: Predefined hook groups to use. Each hook group enables certain builtin hooks that are
  commonly used together. The only hook group available for now is 'C++'.

  'C++'

  : Hooks for converting C++-only headers into C syntax understandable by `cffi`.

load_dump_file

: Save the list of tokens resulting from preprocessing to 'token_dump.pkl'. See save_dump_file
  for more info.

save_dump_file

: Ignore `header_paths` and load the already-preprocessed tokens from 'token_dump.pkl'. This
  can significantly speed up your turnaround time when debugging really large header sets
  when writing and debugging hooks.

# Returns

header_src

: Cleaned header C-code.

macro_rc

: Extracted macros expressed as Python source code.

functions.name: nicelib.process.generate_bindings
documentation:
Generate a skeleton library wrapper.

Grabs all the function declarations from the given header(s), generating a
partially-implemented NiceLib wrapper that can be uncommented and filled in as you go. Supports
NiceObjects and stripping of prefixes.

# Parameters

header_info, kwds :

: These get passed directly to `process_headers()`

outfile

: File (or filename) where output is written.

prefix

: Prefix(es) to strip from toplevel functions.

add_ret_ignore

: Automatically add the 'ignore' return value wrapper for void C functions. False by default.

niceobj_prefix

: Mapping from NiceObject name to its function prefix. If a function has this prefix, it will
  be considered a 'method' of the given NiceObject. These prefixes are checked before the
  top-level prefixes.

fill_handle

: If True, automatically set the first argument of every NiceObject function to `'in'`.
  True by default.

functions.name: nicelib.process.modify_pattern
documentation:
Helper function for easily matching and modifying patterns in a token stream

# Parameters

tokens

: Input token stream to process

pattern

: Pattern to match, as a sequence of (keep, target) pairs. Targets are compared against
  tokens; when the first target is matched, we then try matching the rest in order. If the
  whole pattern is matched, each target's corresponding `keep` indicates whether the token
  should be kept in the sequence, or discarded. `keep` is a string, where 'k' means 'keep',
  'd' means discard and 'a' means add.

  A `target` can be either a string or a `TokenType`.

  There is also some functionality for dealing with blocks enclosed in curly braces {}. For
  more advanced functionality, check out `ParseHelper`.

  Passing a sequence of the type `((keep_start, '{'), ('keep_end', '~~}~~'))` will keep the
  opening `{` according to `keep_start`. `keep_end` is a two letter string, where the
  first letter indicates whether the contents of the block enclosed in braces should be kept,
  and the second indicates if the closing `}` should be kept.
Hooks

functions.name: nicelib.process.remove_pattern
documentation: Convenience function that works like `modify_pattern`, but you only specify targets

functions.name: nicelib.process.add_line_directive_hook
documentation:
Adds line directives to indicate where each token came from

Enabled by default.

functions.name: nicelib.process.declspec_hook
documentation: Removes all occurences of ``__declspec(...)``

functions.name: nicelib.process.cdecl_hook
documentation:
Removes `cdecl`, `_cdecl`, and `__cdecl`

Enabled by default.

functions.name: nicelib.process.inline_hook
documentation: Removes `_inline`, `__inline`, and `__forceinline`

functions.name: nicelib.process.extern_c_hook
documentation: Removes `extern "C" { ... }` while keeping the block's contents

functions.name: nicelib.process.enum_type_hook
documentation: Enum types are now automatically parsed stripped by the CPPParser
Leave this function temporarily for backwards compatibility

functions.name: nicelib.process.asm_hook
documentation: Remove `_asm` and `__asm` and their blocks

functions.name: nicelib.process.stdcall_hook
documentation:
Replace `__stdcall` and `WINAPI` with `volatile volatile const`

Enabled by default.

This technique is stolen from `cffi`.

functions.name: nicelib.process.vc_pragma_hook
documentation: Remove ``__pragma(...)``

functions.name: nicelib.process.struct_func_hook
documentation:
Removes function definitions from inside struct definitions.

Once we're inside the struct's curly braces, we look member-by-member. We push each next token
into a buffer, if we see an open brace then we assume this member is a funcdef. The funcdef is
over after the matching close brace and an optional semicolon. Otherwise, if we see a semicolon
outside of any nesting, that's the end of an ordinary member declaration.

NOTE: Does not deal with nested structs that have methods (if that's even allowed).

If we see a funcdef, throw away all its tokens. If we see a member declaration, pass the tokens
on.

functions.name: nicelib.process.add_typedef_hook
documentation:
Wraps enum/struct/union definitions in a typedef if they lack one

Useful for C++ headers where the typedefs are implicit.
AST Hooks

methods.name: nicelib.process.Lexer.read_token
documentation: Read the next token from text, starting at pos

methods.name: nicelib.process.Parser.assert_line_empty
documentation:
Pops all tokens up to a newline (or end of stream) and asserts that they're empty
(either NEWLINE, WHITESPACE, LINE_COMMENT, or BLOCK_COMMENT)

methods.name: nicelib.process.Parser.pop_until_newline
documentation: Pops all tokens up to a newline (or end of stream) and returns them

classes.name: nicelib.process.TreeModifier
documentation:
A special type of visitor class that modifies an AST in place

Subclass this and implement the various `visit_X` methods which transform nodes of each type.
You can then instantiate the class and use it to implement an AST hook.

Its `visit_X` methods must return a value, which correspond to the transformed node. If the
node is contained in a parent node's list and its `visit_X` method returns None, it will be
removed from the list. This modification/removal is implemented via `generic_visit`, so you can
override it on a per-nodetype basis.

The `X` in `visit_X` is the node type's name, e.g. `visit_Enum`. See `pycparser.c_ast`
or `pycparser._c_ast.cfg` for all the available types of nodes, and
`pycparser.c_ast.NodeVisitor` to see the base visitor class.

classes.name: nicelib.process.FFICleaner

methods.name: nicelib.process.FFICleaner._val_from_const
documentation: Gets the python numeric value of a c_ast Constant (or ID)

A visitor class for cleaning up `c_ast`s

One major feature is that this will evaluate numeric expressions and replace them with
constants. This allows arrays with calculated lengths to be used by cffi. In general we want to
support use of `sizeof` as well, so we need to have ffi parse each typedef as we see it so we
know the available sizes.

This also cleans up a tricky issue where pycparser will split a multi-element typedef into
multiple typedefs, possible duplicating a struct/union/enum definition. Here we are careful to
erase all but the first definition.

All function definitions are also removed.

methods.name: nicelib.process.Generator.common_type_names
documentation:
Get a list of common type names that haven't been typedef'd in the source.

Uses the approach taken from that of cffi's `_common_type_names()`

methods.name: nicelib.process.Generator.parse
documentation: Reimplement CParser.parse to retain scope

classes.name: nicelib.process.ParseHelper

methods.name: nicelib.process.ParseHelper.pop
documentation:
Pop and return next token

Raises StopIteration if we're already at the end of the token stream.

methods.name: nicelib.process.ParseHelper.peek
documentation:
Peek at the next token

Returns None at the end of the token stream.

methods.name: nicelib.process.ParseHelper.peek_true_token
documentation:
Peek at next true token

"True" tokens are non-whitespace and non-comment tokens.

Returns None at the end of the token stream.

methods.name: nicelib.process.ParseHelper.read_until
documentation:
Read until the given token, but do not consume it

Raises StopIteration if we're already at the end of the token stream.

methods.name: nicelib.process.ParseHelper.read_to
documentation:
Read to the given token and consume it

Raises StopIteration if we're already at the end of the token stream.

methods.name: nicelib.process.ParseHelper.read_to_depth
documentation:
Read until the specified nesting depth or the end of the token stream

If `discard` is False, returns a list of the tokens seen before either reaching the
desired depth or end-of-stream.

If `discard` is True, returns True on reaching the desired depth, and raises StopIteration
on end-of-stream.

Always raises StopIteration if we're already at the end of the token stream.

Seeing a `(`, `{`, or `[` increases the depth, and seeing a `)`, `}`, or `]`
decreases it. The current depth is available via the `depth` attribute.

Helper class for processing token streams

Allows you to easily read until specific tokens or the ends of blocks, etc.

classes.name: nicelib.process.CPPTypedefAdder
documentation: Wraps enum/struct/union definitions in a typedef if they lack one
